figures.caption: Table 1: Baseline benchmarks on regular grids (single-input operators). We report the test mean relative ℓ2 error and wall-clock training time under the unified protocol.

Fig. 1: Architecture of the proposed Linear–Nonlinear Fusion Neural Operator (LNF-NO). Each input component (typically a discretized function, e.g., boundary traces or source fields) is encoded separately and concatenated into a latent representation. An operator core then fuses a linear branch and a nonlinear branch via element-wise multiplication (⊙), producing a raw prediction that can be optionally refined by a lightweight decoder. Arrows indicate the data flow.
Table 3: Strongly nonlinear Poisson–Boltzmann regimes (source-free). We vary the nonlinearity strength k in (5) with f = 0 and report test mean relative ℓ2 error and training time.
Table 2: Baseline benchmarks on regular grids (multi-input operators). Each task takes multiple function-valued inputs (e.g., boundary traces and a source field) and predicts fields on a fixed grid.

Table 4: Performance on irregular domains for the Poisson–Boltzmann equation. The problems are posed on non-rectangular geometries with complex boundaries and discretized using unstructured node-based representations, for which grid-based spectral operators such as FNO are not directly applicable.
Fig. 2: 3D Poisson–Boltzmann extension. Central cross-sections of the solution field u(x, y, z) at x = 0.5, y = 0.5, and z = 0.5. The prediction is reconstructed from boundary data by LNF-NO.
